titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the Joseph Longstreth Papers Relating to Caryl Chessman
Joseph Longstreth Papers Relating to Caryl Chessman
titlestmt.author: Sarah Lerner

publicationstmt.publisher: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

maintenanceagency.agencyname: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Sunday July 28, 2019 at 03:00

did.unittitle: Joseph Longstreth papers relating to Caryl Chessman

corpname.part: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

did.abstract: Correspondence, writings, newspaper clippings, and other papers on publishing and legal matters relating to convicted kidnapper and death row inmate Caryl Chessman, collected by editor Joseph Longstreth of Critics Associated, 1953-1990.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: This collection is open for research

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: From the estate of Joseph Longstreth, through Alan Bisbort. Purchased from Lorne Bair Rare Books on the Frederick W. and Carrie S. Beinecke Fund for Western Americana, 2015.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized into five series: I. Correspondence, 1953-1985. II. Newspaper clippings, 1954-1960, undated. III. Other papers, 1953-2015, undated. IV. Writings, 1957-1966, undated. V. Audiovisual materials, 1996, undated.

bioghist.head: Joseph Longstreth ( -2003)
bioghist.p: Joseph Longstreth ( -2003) founded the literary agency Critics Associated. In 1953, he became the editor for Caryl Chessman, convicted robber, kidnapper, and rapist; he later became Chessman's literary executor.

bioghist.head: Caryl Chessman (1921-1960)
bioghist.p: Caryl Chessman (1921-1960) of Saint Joseph, Michigan, was sentenced to death in California for a series of crimes committed in the Los Angeles area in 1948 January. Charged as the "Red Light Bandit", he was convicted under the "Little Lindbergh law", which defined kidnapping as a capital offense under certain circumstances. While spending 12 years on death row, fighting his conviction, Chessman wrote 4 memoirs: Cell 2455 death row (1954), Kid was a killer (1960), The face of justice (1957), and Trial by ordeal (1955).

custodhist.head: Custodial History
custodhist.p: From the estate of Joseph Longstreth, through Caryl Chessman's biographer, Alan Bisbort, author of When you read this, they will have killed me: the life and redemption of Caryl Chessman, whose execution shook America (De Capo Press, 2007).

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Joseph Longstreth Papers Relating to Caryl Chessman. Yale Collection of Western Americana, Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Collections are processed to a variety of levels, depending on the work necessary to make them usable, their perceived research value, the availability of staff, competing priorities, and whether or not further accruals are expected. The library attempts to provide a basic level of preservation and access for all collections, and does more extensive processing of higher priority collections as time and resources permit.

The Joseph Longstreth Papers Relating to Caryl Chessman have been arranged into series, retaining folder titles present at time of acquisition. Several original folders have been seperated into multiple folders for preservation purposes. Original folders with annotations were retained in the collection. Supplied folder titles are bracketed.

scopecontent.head: Content Description
scopecontent.p: Correspondence, writings, newspaper clippings, and other papers on publishing and legal matters relating to convicted kidnapper and death-row inmate Caryl Chessman, collected by editor Joseph Longstreth of Critics Associated, 1953-1990. Includes approximately 2,100 autograph and typescript letters, some signed, 1953-1990. Present are: 400 letters between Chessman and Longstreth discussing finances, publishing matters, and legal proceedings, 1953-1958; correspondence between Longstreth and Chessman's attorney Rosalie Asher, discussing Chessman's life, literary output, finances, mental state, and legal proceedings, 1956-1990; and letters to domestic and foreign publishers, including contracts, royalty statements, legal documents, and marketing and promotional material, 1958-1961. There are also letters addressed to Longstreth from Chessman supporters, 1957-1962. Other papers in the collection include Chessman's typescript appeal to the State Supreme Court of California, undated; 2 short stories, an essay, and an afterword written by Longstreth, undated; and materials pertaining to Chessman's royalties, undated. Collection also includes approximately 750 autograph and typescript pages of unpublished novels and short stories written by Chessman, circa 1948-1960. Accompanied by newspaper clippings about Chessman, 1958-1960, and 2 videocassette tapes: The death penalty (A&E, 1996) and a collection of newscasts about the Chessman case made by Longstreth, undated.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: The Joseph Longstreth Papers Relating to Caryl Chessman is the physical property of the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, Yale University. Literary rights, including copyright, belong to the authors or their legal heirs and assigns. For further information, consult the appropriate curator.

corpname.part: California State Prison at San Quentin

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Series I is arranged alphabetically by individual or subject.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Series I contains correspondence between Joseph Longstreth and Rosalie Asher, Caryl Chessman's laywer, and between Longstreth and Chessman. Letters document discussions of finances, publishing matters, and legal proceedings. Also included is correspondence between Longstreth and Bernice Freeman, a reporter from the San Francisco Chronicle; Frank Olson; historian Harry Elmer Barnes; Frances Couterier, who was romantically involved with Chessman in absentia; and Longstreth's attorney, John Thorne. There are also letters to domestic and foreign publishers, including contracts, royalty statements, legal documents, and marketing and promotional material. Present are letters addressed to Longstreth from Chessman supporters.

did.unittitle: Correspondence: Joseph Longstreth and Rosalie Asher (8 folders)
Trial by ordeal, Chessman correspondence (2 folders)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Series II contains newspaper clippings pertaining to the Chessman case.

did.unittitle: Newspaper clippings related to Chessman (4 folders)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Series III contains financial, legal, and miscellaneous papers pertaining to Caryl Chessman and his published works. There are also publications and ephemera. Included are letters sent from Alan Bisbort, biographer, to Lorne Bair, dealer, explaining how the collection came into his possession.

did.unittitle: Chessman appeal to Supreme Ct. of California
[Alan Bisbort letters to Lorne Bair, dealer]

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Series IV contains manuscript and typescript works of Caryl Chessman and Joseph Longstreth. Some writings are accompanied by correspondence. Included are unpublished materials.

did.unittitle: Art for Arthur's sake, original handwritten story by Caryl Chessman
Autumn and Eve, original handwritten Caryl Chessman story
A corpse for Christmas, by Caryl Chessman
[Corpse for Christmas, Kid was a killer]
Crisis, typescript of story by Caryl Chessman
Dust thou art?, A voice from death row; 2 vignettes by Caryl Chessman
Chapter 28: The face of justice, with annotations by Joseph Longstreth
The gladiator, the disrobing danseuse, and l'amour, typed story by Caryl Chessman
Chessman shorts: The kid was a killer, short story
Pedro was a happy man, story by Caryl Chessman
Young man with a right hand, typed story by Caryl Chessman
Joe Longstreth afterword for Face of justice
Writer: Caryl Chessman, by Joseph Longstreth

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Series V contains 2 videotapes: The death penalty, produced by A&E, 1996; and a collection of recorded newscasts about the Chessman case, made by Joseph Longstreth, undated.
